title: Mechanizing the Odd Order Theorem: Local Analysis
speaker: Georges Gonthier
abstract: Abstract: In addition to formal definitions and theorems, mathematical theories also contain clever, context-sensitive notations, usage conventions, and proof methods. To mechanize advanced mathematical results it is essential to capture these more informal elements. This can be difficult, requiring an array of techniques closer to software engineering than formal logic, but it is essential to obtaining formal proofs of graduate-level mathematics, and can give new insight as well.

proof_stages.label: Formalization goal and Coq infrastructure
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the Odd Order Theorem as a feasible first peak toward mechanizing the classification of finite simple groups and identifies typed logic, reflection, dependent types, canonical structures, type inference, and notation as the needed infrastructure.

proof_stages.label: Global minimal-counterexample roadmap
proof_stages.summary: The speaker outlines the Feit-Thompson strategy: a minimal odd-order simple counterexample is constrained by local analysis, character theory then produces an impossible norm inequality except for a finite-field case, and Galois theory eliminates that case by symmetric inequalities between two primes.

proof_stages.label: Local analysis through uniqueness, transitivity, and p-stability
proof_stages.summary: The local part analyzes Sylow subgroups and their normalizers, showing that the relevant maximal subgroups are mostly of Frobenius type and that the key uniqueness theorem follows from Thompson transitivity and a p-stability consequence valid for odd groups.

proof_stages.label: Coq statements and readable proof scripts for local theorems
proof_stages.summary: The speaker displays Coq formalizations of p-stability, uniqueness, and transitivity and argues that a structured proof language can keep long human-written proof scripts close to the mathematical source while the system fills routine details.

proof_stages.label: Large local-analysis theorem for exceptional maximal subgroups
proof_stages.summary: The actual local analysis requires a large theorem packaging many properties of the non-Frobenius exceptional case, including the existence of a dual exception and the fact that all other maximal subgroups are conjugate to one of the two.

proof_stages.label: Formal idioms for long and non-structural reasoning
proof_stages.summary: To make large theorems usable, the formalization supports standard mathematical idioms such as without-loss-of-generality reductions, overloaded notation for direct sums and presentations, and circular inequality chaining with equality-condition extraction.

proof_stages.label: Methodological lesson for mechanizing big mathematics
proof_stages.summary: The concluding lesson is that large-scale formalization must preserve established notation and proof methods where possible, while using machine support to experiment with and improve proof organization under controlled changes to traditional mathematical style.

examples.name: Four Color Theorem formalization
examples.note: The earlier machine proof of the Four Color Theorem is used as a motivating comparison for why the speaker now tests the same formalization tools on the larger Odd Order Theorem project.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites the Four Color Theorem formalization as a previous project but does no work on it.

examples.note: The speaker explains the rank bound in the uniqueness theorem by noting that rank one would mean a cyclic subgroup, so the actual bound of two is only slightly more complicated.

examples.name: Eigenspace direct-sum notation
examples.note: A Feit-Thompson proof excerpt about eigenspaces covering a vector space is used to show how Coq notation and type inference recover the family underlying a displayed sum.
examples.engagement_reason: He parses the eigenspace sum notation, explains the subspace-sum interpretation and directness predicate, and reasons through how type inference recovers the family.

passes.reason: A direct sum of eigenspaces is the independent/equality case where the subspaces decompose the whole space.

examples.note: The AGM inequality is cited as a familiar case where a chained inequality also carries an equality condition, motivating the circular-inequality toolkit.

examples.name: Rank of a sum of subspaces
examples.note: The inequality that the rank of a sum is at most the sum of the ranks, with equality exactly for a direct sum, exemplifies the formal chaining mechanism used to extract equality consequences.
examples.engagement_reason: He states and applies the rank-of-a-sum inequality with equality iff direct, explaining how it chains inequalities and yields later properties.
